consolidatedPackages.theme: Constraint poisoning from Sequence conformance, cross-module propagation
Consuming, borrowing, iteration, Optional unwrap for ~Copyable
~Escapable accessor, storage, protocol, lazy sequence, pointer patterns

experiments.purpose: Empirically determine whether `var Optional<~Copyable>` can be captured in a non-`@Sendable () -> Void` closure stored as a property of a `~Copyable` Token struct, with `.take()` invoked inside the closure body to consume the captured value exactly once. Triggered by Item 1.5 Phase 2 structural conflict — research doc's Option B shape (explicit `_descriptor: Kernel.Descriptor?` field at L1 swift-memory-primitives) is invalid because L1 cannot import L3 `Kernel` types. Path δ (drop Token's `Sendable` conformance) is the candidate workaround. Tests three scenarios: (1) explicit `release()` then drop, (2) deinit-only release, (3) idempotent double-release. Hypothesis: non-`@Sendable` closure capture of `var Optional<~Copyable>` compiles and runs correctly, releasing the captured value via `.take()` exactly once.
experiments.statusDetail: Hypothesis CONFIRMED in both debug + release builds. Tests 1-3 produce expected output sequences: (1) explicit release invokes closure, `.take()` consumes Resource, deinit fires; subsequent Token deinit no-ops because `_release` was set to nil. (2) deinit-only path fires the closure during Token deinit, `.take()` consumes correctly. (3) double-release is idempotent (second call no-ops). The closure-stored `var captured: Resource?` (Optional ~Copyable) compiles cleanly inside a non-`@Sendable () -> Void` closure type — Swift's `~Copyable` × `@Sendable` capture restriction does NOT apply when the closure is non-`@Sendable`. Trade-off: the Token loses `Sendable` conformance (the closure type itself can't be `@Sendable` if it captures `~Copyable`). Path δ disposition unblocks the L1 Token redesign without requiring Memory.Lock.Token + Memory.Map relocation to L3.

experiments.purpose: Empirically determine which namespace-separation pattern for the Windows-side L2/L3 platform stack compiles cleanly across module boundaries, preserves consumer ergonomics under the cross-platform `Kernel.X` unifier alias, and gives genuine spec-vs-policy separation. Triggered by the Path X close (2026-04-30) and the user direction that future strict-platform compliance phases raw forms out at L2 entirely (typed-only L2 surface). The POSIX-side stack already has `ISO_9945` (IEEE spec root) / `POSIX` (L3-policy / unifier root) as a two-name structure (today typealiased: `POSIX = ISO_9945`); the Windows side has only `Windows`. Five variants tested as 3-target shapes (L2 raw / L3 typed policy / Consumer): V1 status-quo `Windows.Kernel.X` at both L2 and L3; V2 sub-namespace `Windows.ABI.Kernel.X` at L2 + `Windows.Kernel.X` at L3; V3 twin roots `Win32.Kernel.X` at L2 + `Windows.Kernel.X` at L3 (mirrors POSIX shape); V4 org-prefix `Microsoft.Kernel.X` at L2 + `Windows.Kernel.X` at L3; V5 literal-spec `WinSDK.Kernel.X` at L2 + `Windows.Kernel.X` at L3. All 16 targets (5 variants × {L2, L3, Consumer} + SharedHandle utility) build green in debug + release; each consumer's build is itself the cross-module receipt (Consumer → L3 → L2 chain crosses two module boundaries). The differential signal is architectural — spec/policy separation, [API-NAME-003] specification-mirroring fidelity, and Windows-host module-vs-type collision risk. Recommendation document: `swift-institute/Research/windows-l2-l3-namespace-separation.md`.
experiments.statusDetail: V1 (`Windows.Kernel.X` at both L2 and L3) CONFIRMED mechanically with raw `UInt` at L2 and typed `FakeHandle` at L3 — the parameter-type difference resolves the overload ambiguity; REFUTED on spec/policy-separation grounds because there is no namespace-level audit boundary between the two tiers, and the future typed-only L2 phase-out collides with typed-at-L3 at the same syntactic slot per [PLAT-ARCH-008e]. V2 (sub-namespace) CONFIRMED — disjoint extension paths under one `Windows` root; the L2 tier sits at `Windows.ABI.Kernel`, the L3 unifier slot at `Windows.Kernel`. V3 (twin roots) CONFIRMED — entirely disjoint namespace trees; the cleanest spec/policy split and the only variant that mirrors the POSIX-side `ISO_9945`/`POSIX` shape. V4 (org-prefix) CONFIRMED mechanically; sub-optimal because the org-prefix root conflicts with [API-NAME-003] specification-mirroring naming (the ecosystem already roots specs at the spec name — `ISO_9945`, `RFC_4122` — not at the publisher). V5 (literal-spec) CONFIRMED on macOS; ships a latent identifier-overload tax on Windows because `WinSDK` is already the Microsoft-published Swift module name for the Windows SDK headers — Swift modules and top-level types share a single name space at qualified-reference sites, so any consumer reaching for raw C surface will collide with the Swift `enum WinSDK`. The collision cannot fire on macOS or Linux CI; cross-platform CI alone cannot rule out the latent risk. Recommendation: V3.

experiments.purpose: Empirically determine which access-control mechanism supports legitimate raw-FFI use cases without exposing raw shape at any L2 public surface, after the Path X close (2026-04-30) decision to phase out `@_spi(Syscall)` entirely. Six variants tested as 3-target shapes (L2 / L3 / Consumer): V1 status-quo `@_spi(Syscall) public func close(Int32)`; V2 file-scope `private` raw helper inside L2; V3 `internal` raw + sibling target via `@testable import` (V3_L2 compiled with `-enable-testing`); V4 `package` raw form; V5 typed-only L2 with consumer-side raw shim; V6 typed-only at every layer (feasibility baseline). All 18 targets build green in debug + release; cross-module is captured by linking and running each Vk_Consumer (which imports its Vk_L2 + Vk_L3). The differential signal is in the architectural reachability each access-control mechanism provides for the cross-package-stack consumer topology our ecosystem actually has, not in any compile failure. Recommendation document: `swift-institute/Research/spi-syscall-phase-out-layering.md`.
experiments.statusDetail: V1 reachable via `@_spi(Syscall) import`; CONFIRMED mechanically but blocked by user direction (Path X close). V2 raw is invisible to consumers — REFUTED for the consumer-reachable raw use case; CONFIRMED only for in-file raw containment. V3 reachable via `@testable import` only when V3_L2 is compiled with `-enable-testing` — practically refuted at ecosystem scale (compile-flag spread + optimization implications). V4 reachable within one Package.swift, structurally REFUTED across SPM-package boundaries (SE-0386 `package` does not span sibling Package.swift files). V5 reachable via consumer-owned shim, structurally REFUTED by [PLAT-ARCH-008a] (consumers must not bypass the platform stack to import platform C). V6 typed-only is REFUTED — production raw-required use cases (posix_spawn_file_actions, setrlimit / dup2 fd-table manipulation, ABI shims, benchmark bypass) cannot be served without unbounded growth of the typed surface.

experiments.purpose: Approach 1 of the L3-policy-layering investigation (companion to `Research/l3-policy-layering-without-spi-2026-05-02.md`). Test whether dual `internal import L2Methods` + `public import L2Methods` lets L3 see L2's methods internally without re-exporting them to consumers, while still allowing consumers to see types via the public import. Hypothesis: Swift may reject the duplicate import, OR the `public import` may not provide consumer-side type re-export without `@_exported`.
experiments.statusDetail: Swift accepts the dual import syntactically (L3Policy compiles), but `public import L2Methods` does NOT re-export L2Methods's exported symbols (Foo, FooError) into the consumer's name resolution scope — Consumer fails with 'cannot find Foo in scope'. Adding `@_exported` to make consumer see Foo would also re-export L2's `make()` to consumers, returning to approach-8's collision. Swift's import system has no 'types-only re-export' granularity.

experiments.purpose: Approach 2 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. Split L2 into L2Types (struct only) + L2Methods (extension methods). L3 publicly re-exports L2Types via `@_exported public import` so consumers see Foo, but only `internal import L2Methods` so consumers don't see L2's make(). Then L3 declares its own same-signature method.
experiments.statusDetail: Build emits 'function call causes an infinite recursion' warning at the L3 body call site; runtime SIGSEGV (exit 139). The split addresses CONSUMER-side visibility correctly (consumer's `Foo.make()` sees only L3's method) but the L3-internal delegation still fails — inside L3's module, both L2Methods's extension on Foo (via internal import) and L3's local extension are visible, and Swift picks the local declaration as closer candidate. The split is necessary for hiding L2's methods from consumers without `@_spi` but is NOT sufficient for L3-delegates-to-L2.

experiments.purpose: Approach 3 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. Test the @_implementationOnly variant (deprecated since Swift 5.9). Hypothesis: same result as approach 2 — L2 hidden from consumers, but same-signature L3 extension recurses inside L3's body.
experiments.statusDetail: Two warnings: (1) 'function call causes an infinite recursion' at the L3 body call (same defect as approach 8); (2) [#ImplementationOnlyDeprecated] — the compiler steers users to `internal import` instead. Runtime: SIGSEGV (exit 139). @_implementationOnly is the older, stricter form of `internal import`, behaviorally identical for our case. Doubly disqualified: same defect AS approach 8 PLUS deprecated mechanism.

experiments.purpose: Approach 4 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. Test whether @_disfavoredOverload on L2's same-signature method lets L3's local declaration win in overload resolution AND lets L3's body call resolve to L2 (instead of self-recursing). Hypothesis: @_disfavoredOverload addresses preference, not within-module disambiguation.
experiments.statusDetail: Same 'infinite recursion' warning at L3Policy/Foo+policy.swift; runtime SIGSEGV (exit 139). @_disfavoredOverload addresses overload resolution PREFERENCE (consumers pick L3 over L2's disfavored method) but does NOT change the fact that within L3's module, L3's own non-disfavored declaration is the closest candidate — the body call still resolves to L3 itself. The disfavored modifier helps consumers but not the L3-internal delegation step.

experiments.purpose: Approach 5 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. L3-policy methods live at Foo.Policy.* sub-namespace, not on Foo directly. Eliminates same-signature same-nominal-type collision. Trade-off: consumer migration required (call shape changes from `Foo.make()` to `Foo.Policy.make()`).
experiments.statusDetail: Build clean; runtime success: `Foo.make()` returns L2 value, `Foo.Policy.make()` returns L3-wrapped 'L3.Policy(L2)'. Passes criteria (1)(3)(4)(6); FAILS criterion (5) — consumers must migrate every `Foo.make()` to `Foo.Policy.make()` to opt into L3 policy. Useful as fallback if no criterion-(5)-respecting alternative survives principal review.

experiments.purpose: Approach 6 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. Test whether protocol-extension dispatch can express L3-policy while preserving consumer call shape. Hypothesis: protocol extensions do NOT shadow type-extension methods of the same name; protocol must use a DIFFERENT method name.
experiments.statusDetail: Build clean; runtime success when called via the protocol method name (`Foo.makeWithPolicy()` returns 'L3-protocol(L2)'). Type-extension methods take priority over protocol-extension defaults at dispatch — protocol extensions are LATE-RESOLUTION fallbacks. So the protocol approach can only deliver L3 policy via a DIFFERENT method name. Equivalent to approach 5 in trade-off: criterion (5) violated.

experiments.purpose: Approach 7 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. Investigate whether rule-legal-demo or rule-law-demo employ a layering pattern matching the L2/L3 same-name method-wrapping problem. Documentation-only experiment (grep-based investigation).
experiments.statusDetail: Grep across rule-legal-demo and rule-law-demo found no @_spi, no @_implementationOnly, no @_disfavoredOverload — only @_exported import for the standard re-export chain pattern ([PLAT-ARCH-006]). The legal architecture's four layers (Namespace, Legislature, Judiciary, Composition, Products) compose ADDITIVELY: Layer N adds NEW types/methods on NEW namespaces; never overrides Layer N-1's same-signature method on the same type. The recalled precedent does not address the same-name method-wrapping problem.

experiments.purpose: Approach 8 of the L3-policy-layering investigation. Verify that the existing Wave 3.5-Corrective '@_spi(Syscall) at L2 + cross-module same-signature L3 extension' pattern works in minimal form. This is the BASELINE — the dispatched pattern at iso-9945 commits 2aed9ad / cb053e5 + swift-posix commits 0c3545a / d8c5877.
experiments.statusDetail: CRITICAL FINDING: The Wave 3.5-Corrective pattern is structurally broken at runtime. Build emits 'function call causes an infinite recursion' warning at L3Policy/Foo+policy.swift:5; runtime SIGSEGV (exit 139). Within L3Policy's module, the call `try Foo.make()` overload-resolves to L3Policy's OWN local extension declaration (same-signature, same nominal type), not to L2Methods's @_spi(Syscall) declaration. @_spi affects consumer-facing visibility (consumer without @_spi import doesn't see L2 method) but does NOT affect overload resolution within L3's module — both declarations are equally-ranked candidates and Swift prefers the local. PRODUCTION VERIFICATION: swift-foundations/swift-posix/Sources/POSIX Kernel File/POSIX.Kernel.File.Open.swift:97 exhibits the SAME warning under clean build. The Wave 3.5-Corrective-2 commit d8c5877 landed broken delegation; the Stats analogue (commit 0c3545a) likely shares the defect. Tests likely don't exercise the policy path at runtime, hiding the regression.

experiments.purpose: Approach 9 of the L3-policy-layering investigation — NEW pattern surfaced during analysis. L2 hosts its typed methods at a SUB-NAMESPACE under Foo (Foo.Syscall.make()), NOT at Foo directly. L3 declares its user-facing method at Foo.make() directly. Variant of [PLAT-ARCH-008e] Phase A rename pattern, applied via sub-namespace nesting rather than method renaming. Structurally identical to [PLAT-ARCH-008f] solution family (c) 'Sub-namespace on L2'.
experiments.statusDetail: Passes ALL six criteria: type identity preserved, L3-policy resolution at consumer site (`Foo.make()` returns 'L3(L2.Syscall)'), type instantiation works, NO @_spi (zero @_spi attributes), NO consumer call shape change (`Foo.make()` exactly as before), compiles cleanly (no warnings). Power-user access to raw L2 form is at `Foo.Syscall.make()` — equivalent in spirit to @_spi(Syscall) but via namespace nesting rather than visibility attribute. Build clean; runtime: `Foo.make() = L3(L2.Syscall)` (L3 wraps and delegates), `Foo.Syscall.make() = L2.Syscall` (raw access), exit 0. POST-PRINCIPAL-FEEDBACK STATUS: demoted to FALLBACK. Principal reframed the problem (2026-05-02, same session): the existing ISO_9945.* / POSIX.* top-level namespaces already provide L2/L3 separation, making the .Syscall. sub-namespace structurally redundant. Approach 10 (distinct top-level types, no typealias chain) is the primary recommendation; approach 9 is preserved as fallback for the case where some downstream constraint requires a single nominal type at the L2/L3 boundary. Recommendation document: `swift-institute/Research/l3-policy-layering-without-spi-2026-05-02.md`.

experiments.purpose: Approach 10 of the L3-policy-layering investigation — PRIMARY RECOMMENDATION post-principal-feedback. Drop the typealias `POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats = ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats` entirely. Let `ISO_9945.*` and `POSIX.*` be DISTINCT top-level namespaces, each owning its own type definitions and methods. iso-9945 codes the spec-literal POSIX `struct stat` mirror naturally with no awareness of downstream layers (no @_spi, no sub-namespace, no disambiguation modifiers). swift-posix declares its own distinct `POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats` struct with whatever field shape and policy methods it deems appropriate. Conversion at the L2/L3 boundary is internal to swift-posix's body via `internal init(from l2: ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats)`. Type identity at the consumer level is preserved by the L3-unifier typealias `Kernel.File = POSIX.Kernel.File` (cross-platform unifier namespace, not L2/L3-boundary). Mirrors the structural shape Wave 3.5-1..8 already established for the 37 non-corrective namespaces (distinct enum at POSIX), generalised to struct-shape for the 4 corrective namespaces.
experiments.statusDetail: Passes ALL six criteria: (1) Type identity preserved at consumer (Kernel.File.Stats resolves through Kernel.File = POSIX.Kernel.File to a usable struct); (2) L3-policy resolution at consumer (Kernel.File.Stats.get() routes to POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats.get(), which delegates to L2 cleanly — different nominal types, no overload collision); (3) Type instantiation works; (4) NO @_spi (zero @_spi attributes anywhere); (5) NO consumer call shape change (Kernel.File.Stats.get() identical to single-module form); (6) Compiles cleanly (no warnings). Build clean; runtime: ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats.get() = size=1024 perms=644, POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats.get() = size=1024 perms=644, Kernel.File.Stats.get() = size=1024 perms=644 (via Kernel.File = POSIX.Kernel.File flip), exit 0. Architectural alignment: same structural shape as the 37 non-corrective Wave 3.5 namespaces; no typealias chain to break overload resolution; no `.Syscall.` sub-namespace required at L2 because the natural ISO_9945.* namespace already serves that role. User framing realised: 'lower level (l2) coded without regard to downstream' — iso-9945 codes ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats naturally, no choices made for downstream's benefit; 'upstream presents its public API as it wants' — swift-posix declares POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats with whatever field shape, accessors, and policy methods it deems appropriate. Recommendation document: `swift-institute/Research/l3-policy-layering-without-spi-2026-05-02.md` (REVISION NOTE section + approach 10 primary recommendation).

experiments.purpose: Approach 11 of the L3-policy-layering investigation — STRUCTURALLY GENERALIZABLE PATTERN matching the rule-law-us-nv legal architecture. Operations are encoded as STRUCTS whose init does the work (mirroring `\`NRS 77\`.\`310\`.\`1\`.init(...)` in `rule-law-us-nv/Sources/Rule Law US Nevada/Rule Law US Nevada.swift`); the same struct also carries result fields. L3 override is achieved by replacing `POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats = ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats` typealias with a distinct WRAPPING struct that holds L2's struct as `private let _underlying: ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats` (single source of truth for data; field accessors forward to _underlying). Ergonomic method `Kernel.File.Stats.get(...)` is added at the L3-unifier ONLY (single extension site, no cross-module collision). Body of the unifier method calls `Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor:)` (a TYPE INIT, not a method call) which resolves through the typealias chain to POSIX's policy init.
experiments.statusDetail: Passes ALL six criteria: (1) Type identity preserved at consumer via Kernel.File.Stats = POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats typealias chain; data is single-source at L2 via _underlying stored property (no field duplication, maintenance changes propagate). (2) L3-policy resolution at consumer: Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor: -1) returns size=1024 (L3 retry kicked in) while ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: -1) THROWS interrupted (L2 has no retry) — proves L3's policy is reached. (3) Type instantiation works. (4) NO @_spi anywhere. (5) NO consumer call shape change (Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor:) is the user-facing entry). (6) Compiles cleanly (no warnings). Build clean; runtime: 'Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor: 0) = size=1024 perms=644', 'Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor: -1) = size=1024 perms=644 (L3 policy retry kicked in)', 'ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: -1) THROWS interrupted', 'POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: -1) = size=1024', exit 0. Mechanism: `static func get(...)` and `init(descriptor:)` are different declaration kinds — Swift's overload resolution doesn't conflate them; body call `Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: ...)` is a TYPE INIT, not a method call, so no recursion shape exists. Override-toggle: no override needed = `public typealias Stats = ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats` (single-line trivial case); override needed = distinct wrapping struct (this experiment). Generalizable: same pattern works for the legal architecture (already in production at rule-law-us-nv) and for syscall layering. Addresses principal's 'duplicate structs is a deal breaker' concern at the data-fields level via wrap-as-stored-property (data is single-source at L2; only the operation type is distinct). Recommendation document: `swift-institute/Research/l3-policy-layering-without-spi-2026-05-02.md`. Production precedent: `rule-law-us-nv/Sources/Rule Law US Nevada/Rule Law US Nevada.swift` lines 41-57 ('replace the typealias with a custom type that wraps or extends the statute encoding').

experiments.purpose: Approach 12 of the L3-policy-layering investigation — REFINEMENT of approach 11 using `swift-tagged-primitives.Tagged` with the L3 namespace enum as the phantom tag and L2's struct as the RawValue. Principal-suggested formulation (verbatim): `public typealias \`POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats\` = Tagged<POSIX, ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats>`. L2 (iso-9945) stays unchanged from production form (zero migration cost at L2); L3 (swift-posix) wraps via Tagged with the L3 namespace as discriminator. Operations live in constrained extensions on Tagged (`extension Tagged where Tag == POSIX, RawValue == ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats { init(descriptor:) ... static func get(...) ... }`). L3-unifier flip `Kernel.File.Stats = POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats` resolves through to the Tagged variant.
experiments.statusDetail: Passes ALL six criteria: (1) Type identity preserved at consumer via Tagged generic instantiation; single nominal type at consumer (Kernel.File.Stats = Tagged<POSIX, ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats>); single source of data via Tagged.rawValue. (2) L3-policy resolution at consumer: Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor: -1) succeeds via L3 retry while ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: -1) throws — proves L3's constrained-extension init is dispatched through the typealiased site. (3) Type instantiation works (Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor:), .get(descriptor:), and `let s: Kernel.File.Stats = ...` all valid). (4) NO @_spi anywhere. (5) NO consumer call shape change. (6) Compiles cleanly (no warnings). Build clean; runtime: 'Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor: 0) = size=1024 perms=644', 'Kernel.File.Stats.get(descriptor: -1) = size=1024 perms=644 (L3 policy retry kicked in)', 'ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: -1) THROWS interrupted', 'POSIX.Kernel.File.Stats(descriptor: -1) = size=1024 (L3 init handles retry via Tagged constrained ext)', 'withRetry.rawValue: size=1024 (L2 struct accessible via .rawValue)', exit 0. Architectural advantages over approaches 10/11: (a) L2 stays unchanged — iso-9945's struct shape identical to current production, zero L2 migration cost (vs approach 11's struct-init paradigm shift); (b) single source of data via Tagged.rawValue — adding a field at L2 propagates automatically; (c) phantom tag = L3 namespace enum itself (no new tag types); (d) constrained-extension dispatch on Tagged. Trade-offs: (a) field-access ergonomics — forwarding accessors needed at L3 for consumers wanting `stats.size` rather than `stats.rawValue.size`; (b) swift-tagged-primitives becomes load-bearing dependency for the L2/L3 platform stack; (c) distinct nominal types still exist (Tagged<POSIX, X> ≠ X) — same structural property as approaches 10/11 expressed via generic instantiation rather than hand-written wrapping struct.

experiments.purpose: Approach 13 of the L3-policy-layering investigation — GENERALIZATION of approach 12 leveraging swift-carrier-primitives' Carrier protocol. Principal observation (verbatim): 'we could then also use carrier-primitives (tagged conforms to carrier) and use some Carrier<X> to allow both L2 and L3 types where it doesn't matter'. Verified at the source: Tagged+Carrier.swift:18-21 explicitly states this is the design intent ('This is the move that lets `some Carrier<Cardinal>` accept bare `Cardinal`, `Tagged<User, Cardinal>`, and any further-nested Tagged variant uniformly'). L2's plain struct conforms to Carrier as trivial self-carrier (one-line opt-in: `extension ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats: Carrier { typealias Underlying = Self }` with default impls from `Carrier where Underlying == Self.swift`). Tagged<POSIX, ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats>'s Carrier conformance cascades via Tagged+Carrier (Underlying = RawValue.Underlying). Net effect: `func describeStats(_: some Carrier<ISO_9945.Kernel.File.Stats>)` accepts BOTH the bare L2 struct AND the L3 Tagged variant uniformly, AND the Kernel.File.Stats unifier name (which resolves through the typealias chain to Tagged).
experiments.statusDetail: Passes mechanically. Build clean (no warnings); runtime: 'L2 bare struct via describeStats: size=1024 perms=644', 'L3 Tagged variant via describeStats: size=1024 perms=644', 'Kernel.File.Stats (= L3 Tagged) via describeStats: size=1024 perms=644', 'L2.underlying type matches L3.underlying type: true', 'type(of: l2): Stats', 'type(of: l3): Tagged<POSIX, Stats>', exit 0. What this confirms: (a) trivial self-carrier conformance at L2 is mechanically free (defaults from Carrier where Underlying == Self.swift provide var underlying and init(_:) automatically); (b) Tagged's existing Carrier conformance cascades correctly with no additional plumbing at swift-posix; (c) `some Carrier<X>` is the lightweight generic syntax (Underlying is primary associated type on Carrier); (d) static type identity is preserved (l2 is Stats; l3 is Tagged<POSIX, Stats>) — distinct nominal types remain available for code that needs to distinguish layers; (e) layer-agnostic processing is now expressible at any abstraction level — utility functions taking `some Carrier<L2.Type>` operate on the canonical underlying data regardless of layer. Architectural value beyond approach 12: approach 12 unified the consumer call shape via L3-unifier typealias; approach 13 extends that to the GENERIC API LEVEL — code processing 'any Stats' across libraries, layers, nested Tagged variants is a single generic function. This is what 'ecosystem-wide layer-agnostic helpers' looks like in practice.

experiments.purpose: Minimal reproducer for a Swift 6.3.1 / 6.4-dev release-mode miscompile: a raw pointer captured via `withUnsafePointer(to: value) { $0 }` with `value: borrowing T` for `T: ~Copyable`, stored in an `@inlinable`-init `~Escapable` wrapper under `@_lifetime(borrow value)`, becomes a dangling pointer after the init returns. Release-mode `.value` reads return garbage or crash. The sibling `inout value: inout Value` + `withUnsafeMutablePointer(to: &value)` pattern works correctly with the same storage shape — proving the bug is specific to the borrowing overload when inlined at `-O`. No package imports; pure stdlib + experimental Lifetimes features. Nine variants discriminate fix hypotheses (~Copyable wrapper, @_addressableForDependencies, _overrideLifetime, heap-owned class, caller-scoped pointer, inout). A minimum-bar single-file companion at `minimal-repro.swift` (adjacent to Package.swift, outside Sources/) contrasts the two core variants in ~100 lines and is suitable for direct attachment to a swiftlang/swift bug report.
experiments.statusDetail: V5 (heap-owned class, Copyable Value): PASS. V4 (caller-scoped typed init via withUnsafePointer): PASS. V8 (inout + withUnsafeMutablePointer): PASS — proves the stored-pointer pattern works with the inout ABI. V7 (_overrideLifetime + ~Escapable carrier): CRASH. V6 (@_addressableForDependencies on Value type): CRASH. V3 (~Copyable wrapper): CRASH. V1/V2 (baselines, ~Copyable and Copyable Value): CRASH. Separately observed: removing @inlinable from the borrowing init and calling it from a different module preserves the @in_guaranteed indirect ABI that inlining at -O loses. This file is single-module, so that route is not exercised here.

experiments.purpose: Measure the per-init performance cost of the `@inlinable`-removal workaround applied to `Ownership.Borrow.init(borrowing:)` and `Property.View.@unsafe init(_ base: borrowing Base)` (swift-ownership-primitives `ece5d7e`, swift-property-primitives `764db07`) to rescue cross-module consumers from the release-mode miscompile documented in `borrow-pointer-storage-release-miscompile`. Two-module setup (`Wrappers` library + `Benchmark` executable) with `InlineWrapper` (@inlinable init) and `OutOfLineWrapper` (non-@inlinable init) of identical one-line bodies (`withUnsafeMutablePointer(to: &value) { UnsafeMutableRawPointer($0) }`) called in a tight loop. Uses `inout` parameter shape (not `borrowing`) since the `borrowing` variant crashes under @inlinable — the calling-convention cost measured here is architecturally equivalent to the borrowing-parameter case.
experiments.statusDetail: Realistic hot-path overhead ≈ 3.5 ns per non-inlined wrapper construction (Apple Silicon, macOS .v26, release mode, 500M iterations). Loop B (opaque write + wrapper + load): Direct 0.806 ns/iter, InlineWrapper 0.808 ns/iter (fully elided — optimizer sees through the @inlinable body), OutOfLineWrapper 4.354 ns/iter. Delta ≈ 3.5 ns ≈ 12 CPU cycles at 3.5 GHz — firmly in the function-call-overhead regime, consistent with the workaround replacing an inlined body with a cross-module call. Loop A (no load, DCE-friendly): inlined 0 ns (fully eliminated), non-inlined 5.5 ns — confirms the upper bound when the wrapper's only use is DCE-able. Interpretation: the absolute overhead is bounded; relative overhead depends on surrounding work. For Property.View hot paths with ~50–100 ns of real work per view, the workaround costs <5% relative. For synthetic tight loops doing only pointer loads through views, the overhead approaches 5x vs direct access.

experiments.purpose: Domain-free minimal reproducer for the overload-resolution footgun where `(0..<5).map(Target.init)` silently routes through `ExpressibleByIntegerLiteral + Strideable` on an intermediate type to select an unlabeled cross-domain init. Four variants confirm (1) the footgun fires in pure-algebra form, (2) `@_disfavoredOverload` provides no protection, (3) removing `Strideable` breaks the chain, (4) explicit cross-domain invocation produces the same (correct) scaling semantics.
experiments.statusDetail: The hazard reproduces with plain structs (no Tagged, no phantom types). Ingredients: (a) intermediate type conforms to ExpressibleByIntegerLiteral, (b) intermediate type conforms to Strideable, (c) target's ONLY single-arg init is unlabeled cross-domain init(_: Intermediate), (d) `.map(Target.init)` call site with a literal Range. Caveat surfaced during construction: labeled inits ARE visible to `.map(Type.init)` function references (labels are part of the init name, not the function-value type), so the footgun requires NO direct (ContextType) -> Target path to exist alongside the cross-domain one.

experiments.purpose: Companion package to the blog post 'Designing namespaced accessors in Swift' (BLOG-IDEA-061). Five variants walk from a single bespoke proxy struct on a Copyable Stack to a pointer-backed Wrapper.View on a ~Copyable Ring, each variant demonstrating one step in the discovery of the Property<Tag, Base> shape provided by swift-property-primitives.
experiments.statusDetail: All five variants build and run on Swift 6.3.1. V1_BespokeProxy demonstrates the five-step _modify dance on a single Copyable proxy. V2_FiveProxies shows the structural duplication across five proxies on the same Stack. V3_Wrapper factors the tag into a generic parameter (method-case extensions only; property-case motivates Property.Typed, out of scope for the walkthrough). V4_NoncopyableFails compiles with the failing accessor commented out; uncommenting reproduces the error that ~Copyable 'self' cannot be partially consumed and reinitialized (the five-step dance requires Copyable storage). V5_View demonstrates Wrapper.View backed by UnsafeMutablePointer yielded from mutating _read / _modify coroutines on the ~Copyable Ring — both accessor forms are required for full call-site mutation semantics. Gotcha surfaced during construction: `extension Wrapper { struct View: ~Copyable, ~Escapable { ... } }` implicitly adds `where Base: Copyable`; the View must be declared inside `extension Wrapper where Base: ~Copyable { ... }` to be usable on ~Copyable bases.

experiments.purpose: Swift 6.3.1 SendNonSendable SIL pass aborts with signal 6 when a function body wraps the construction of a cross-module `~Copyable, ~Escapable` View (via a `@_lifetime(borrow base) init(_ base: borrowing Base)`) inside an IIFE. Minimal reducer: 10 lines across two modules. Workaround: bind the view at the outer scope — the IIFE is the load-bearing trigger.
experiments.statusDetail: STILL CRASHES as of Swift 6.3.1. Crash: swift::Partition::merge in SendNonSendable::run(). Minimum ingredients: Target: ~Copyable, View<Base>: ~Copyable+~Escapable with @_lifetime(borrow base) borrowing init, cross-module, IIFE, function-body (not top-level), Lifetimes feature flag.

narrativeSections.heading: Bug: ~Copyable Inline Storage Deinit (Swift Compiler Bug)
narrativeSections.body:
**Location**: `noncopyable-inline-deinit/`

**Symptom**: ~Copyable structs fail to call element deinitializers when destroyed. Elements are leaked.

### Root Cause: Precise Trigger Conditions

The bug is triggered by this **exact combination**:

1. `InlineArray<capacity, ...>` where `capacity` is a **value generic parameter** (not a literal)
2. ~Copyable struct containing only value-type properties
3. **Cross-module boundary**: Element type (e.g., `TrackedElement`) defined in a different module than the container
4. deinit that performs manual element cleanup

### Isolation Test Results

| Configuration | Deinit Called? |
|---------------|----------------|
| `InlineArray<4, ...>` (literal capacity) | ✅ YES |
| Value generic `<let capacity: Int>` without InlineArray | ✅ YES |
| `InlineArray<capacity, ...>` with value generic | ❌ NO (BUG) |
| Same + `var _deinitWorkaround: AnyObject? = nil` | ✅ YES |

### Minimal Reproduction

```swift
// In Module A (ContainerLib):
public struct Container<Element: ~Copyable, let capacity: Int>: ~Copyable {
    var _storage: InlineArray<capacity, (Int, Int, Int, Int, Int, Int, Int, Int)>
    var _count: Int
    // NO reference type properties

    deinit {
        // This code path is never executed for cross-module ~Copyable elements
        for i in 0..<_count {
            // deinitialize elements...
        }
    }
}

// In Module B (Tests):
struct TrackedElement: ~Copyable {
    deinit { print("deinit called") }  // NEVER PRINTED
}

var container = Container<TrackedElement, 4>()
container.push(TrackedElement())
// container goes out of scope - TrackedElement.deinit NOT called
```

### Workaround

Add a reference type property to the struct:

```swift
var _deinitWorkaround: AnyObject? = nil
```

This forces the compiler to generate correct deinit dispatch.

### NOT Contributing Factors

These were tested and do NOT affect the bug:
- `@inlinable` / `@usableFromInline` attributes
- Nesting inside a generic outer container
- Whether outer container is generic or not
- Ring buffer logic / modulo calculations
- `withUnsafeBytes` vs `withUnsafePointer` pattern

### Affected Packages

| Package | Type | Status |
|---------|------|--------|
| swift-deque-primitives | `Deque.Inline` | Fixed (workaround applied) |
| swift-queue-primitives | `Queue.Inline` | Fixed (workaround applied) |
| swift-stack-primitives | `Stack.Inline` | Fixed (workaround applied) |

### Filing Bug Report

This experiment provides a minimal reproduction case suitable for a Swift compiler bug report:

```
noncopyable-inline-deinit/
├── Package.swift
├── Sources/ContainerLib/Container.swift  (library with bug trigger)
└── Tests/ContainerTests.swift            (reproduction tests)
```

Run `swift test --filter "Critical"` to demonstrate the bug.

**TODO**: File Swift compiler bug report with this reproduction case.

narrativeSections.heading: Issue: ~Copyable Constraint Poisoning (Compiler Limitation)
narrativeSections.body:
**Related experiments**: `noncopyable-pointer-propagation`, `noncopyable-storage-poisoning`, `noncopyable-multifile-poisoning`, `noncopyable-sequence-protocol-test`

**Research paper**: `Noncopyable Generics Constraint Propagation.md`

**Symptom**: Adding a conditional conformance `where Element: Copyable` causes stored properties using `Element` to fail with "type 'Element' does not conform to protocol 'Copyable'"—even when those properties (like `UnsafeMutablePointer<Element>`) explicitly support ~Copyable elements.

### Root Cause

When a type `T<E: ~Copyable>` gains a conformance with `where E: Copyable`, Swift's type checker propagates the `Copyable` constraint backwards to the type definition. This "poisons" stored properties:

```swift
struct Container<Element: ~Copyable>: ~Copyable {
    var storage: UnsafeMutablePointer<Element>  // ❌ Poisoned by conformance below
}

extension Container: Sequence where Element: Copyable {
    // This conformance causes the error above
}
```

### What Does NOT Prevent Poisoning

| Approach | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Conformance in separate file | ❌ Still poisons |
| Conformance in same file | ❌ Still poisons |
| Custom protocol instead of Swift.Sequence | ❌ Still poisons |
| Protocol without `associatedtype Element` | ✅ Works (but loses Sequence) |
| Conditional `Copyable` on the type itself | ❌ Still poisons |

### What DOES Prevent Poisoning

| Approach | Result |
|----------|--------|
| **Separate SPM module** | ✅ Works |
| **Wrapper type** | ✅ Works (less ergonomic) |

### Solution: Module Boundary Isolation

Module boundaries are real compilation boundaries. The compiler processes each target independently:

```
Package/
├── Sources/
│   ├── Core/           # Type with ~Copyable support, NO Sequence conformance
│   │   └── Container.swift
│   ├── Sequence/       # Conformances for Copyable elements
│   │   └── Container+Sequence.swift  (imports Core)
│   └── Public/         # Re-exports both
│       └── exports.swift
```

When Sequence module adds conformances, Core has already been compiled—its stored properties validated without the Copyable constraint.

### Applied Solution

| Package | Implementation |
|---------|----------------|
| swift-array-primitives | Split into Core/Sequence internal modules |

### Wrapper Alternative

When module splitting is impractical, wrapper types avoid direct conformance:

```swift
extension Container where Element: Copyable {
    public var iterable: IterableView { ... }
}
for x in container.iterable { }  // Not `for x in container`
```

Trade-off: Requires `.iterable` accessor; copies elements upfront.

### Related Swift Evolution

- **SE-0427**: Noncopyable Generics (implemented, constraint propagation is intended)
- **SE-0437**: Noncopyable Stdlib Primitives (UnsafeMutablePointer supports ~Copyable)
- **Suppressed Associated Types Pitch**: Would solve this if accepted (not available today)
